en.extract:
The Sri Lanka shama (Copsychus leggei) is a medium-sized passerine bird in the Old World flycatcher family Muscicapidae that is endemic to Sri Lanka. It was formerly considered to be conspecific with the white-rumped shama.


Taxonomy
The Sri Lanka shama was formally described in 1941 by the English ornithologist Hugh Whistler as a subspecies of the white-rumped shama. He coined the trinomial name Kittacincla malabarica leggei where the epithet leggei was chosen to honour the memory of the ornithologist William Vincent Legge who had documented the birds of Sri Lanka. The Sri Lanka shama is now placed with 16 other species in the genus Copsychus  that was introduced in 1827 by the German naturalist Johann Georg Wagler. It was formerly considered as subspecies of the white-rumped shama (Copsychus malabaricus) but is now treated as a separate species based on morphological differences and a molecular genetic study of museum specimens published in 2022. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
The Sri Lanka shama has glossy blue-black upperparts, bright chestnut underparts, a white rump and white outer tail feathers. The sexes are similar. Compared to the nominate subspecies of white-rumped shama that is present in India, the Sri Lanka shama has a shorter tail, paler belly and less sexual dimorphism.

The '''Sri Lanka shama''' ('''''Copsychus leggei''''') is a medium-sized [[passerine]] bird in the Old World flycatcher family [[Muscicapidae]] that is endemic to Sri Lanka. It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[white-rumped shama]].

==Taxonomy==
The Sri Lanka shama was [[species description|formally described]] in 1941 by the English ornithologist [[Hugh Whistler]] as a [[subspecies]] of the [[white-rumped shama]]. He coined the [[trinomial name]] ''Kittacincla malabarica leggei'' where the epithet ''leggei'' was chosen to honour the memory of the ornithologist [[William Vincent Legge]] who had documented the birds of Sri Lanka.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Whistler | first=H. | author-link=Hugh Whistler | date=1941 | title=Recognition of new subspecies of birds in Ceylon | journal=Ibis | volume=82 | issue=2 | pages=319-320 [319] | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1941.tb00626.x }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Paynter | editor2-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1964 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=10 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=69 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14486258 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=leggei | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=leggei | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=13 November 2024 }}</ref> The Sri Lanka shama is now placed with 16 other species in the genus ''[[Copsychus ]]'' that was introduced in 1827 by the German naturalist [[Johann Georg Wagler]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Chats, Old World flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/chats/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=14 November 2024}}</ref> It was formerly considered as [[subspecies]] of the [[white-rumped shama]] (''Copsychus malabaricus'') but is now treated as a separate species based on morphological differences and a molecular genetic study of museum specimens published in 2022.<ref name=ioc/><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Wu | first1=M.Y. | last2=Lau | first2=C.J. | last3=Ng | first3=E.Y.X. | last4=Baveja | first4=P. | last5=Gwee | first5=C.Y. | last6=Sadanandan | first6=K. | last7=Ferasyi | first7=T.R. | last8=Haminuddin | last9=Ramadhan | first9=R. | last10=Menner | first10=J.K. | last11=Rheindt | first11=F.E. | date=2022 | title=Genomes from historic DNA unveil massive hidden extinction and terminal endangerment in a tropical Asian songbird radiation | journal=Molecular Biology and Evolution | volume=39 | issue=9 | article-number=msac189 | doi=10.1093/molbev/msac189 | doi-access=free | pmc=9486911 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>

==Description==
The Sri Lanka shama has glossy blue-black upperparts, bright chestnut underparts, a white rump and white outer tail feathers. The sexes are similar. Compared to the [[nominate subspecies]] of white-rumped shama that is present in India, the Sri Lanka shama has a shorter tail, paler belly and less [[sexual dimorphism]].<ref name=bow>{{cite web | last1=Collar | first1=N. | last2=Kirwan | first2=G.M. | date=2024 | title=Sri Lankan Shama (''Copsychus leggei''), version 1.0 | editor1-last=Keeney | editor1-first=B.K. | editor2-last=Billerman | editor2-first=S.M. | work=Birds of the World | location=Ithaca, NY, USA | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | url=https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.whrsha5.01 | access-date=14 November 2024 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>
